1: bĕkā ḥāsîtî — in you I have taken refuge. The qal perfect ḥāsāh expresses completed, settled trust; the Psalmist does not come to refuge now under new pressure but arrives already sheltered. The opening claim is the ground, not the plea — the prayer begins from within the refuge, not en route to it.
2: haṭṭēh ʾêlay ʾoznĕkā — incline your ear to me. The imperative haṭṭēh (hiphil of nāṭāh: to stretch, incline) pictures God physically bending toward the one praying — a gesture of intimate, condescending attention. Combined with hôshîʿēnî (save me) and ûpallĕṭēnî (deliver me), three imperatives arrive in rapid succession, intensifying the urgency.
3: ṣûr māʿôn — a sheltering rock. The noun māʿôn (dwelling place, permanent habitation) joins ṣûr (rock) to image God not merely as a temporary shelter but as a perpetual home. The clause ṣiwwîtā lĕhôshîaʿ (you have commanded to save) grounds the petition in God's own prior decree — the Psalmist appeals to what God himself has already ordained.
4: ûmiyyad ʿawwēl — from the hand of the unjust. Three terms accumulate: rāshāʿ (wicked), ʿawwēl (unjust, one who wrests things from their right order), and ḥōmēṣ (oppressor, a violent seizer). The trio characterizes the enemy by moral category before any specific action is named — the adversary is defined by what he fundamentally is, not merely what he does.
5: kî-ʾattāh tiqwātî — for you are my hope. Tiqwāh literally means a cord or thread (as in Rahab's scarlet cord, Joshua 2:18) before it means hope — a hope that is a tangible line connecting the Psalmist to God. Mibbĕṭuḥî minnĕʿûrāy — my trust from my youth. The personal history of trust stretches back to adolescence; the prayer of old age stands on decades of tested reliance.
6: ʿālêkā nismaktî mibbāṭen — upon you I have leaned from the womb. The niphal of sāmak (to lean, support oneself on another) is rare in this personal sense. Mibbāṭen (from the belly/womb) pushes the origin of trust before birth itself — the Psalmist's dependence on God predates conscious memory. This is unique in the Psalter as an explicit claim of in-utero divine sustaining; it anticipates Jeremiah's call narrative (Jeremiah 1:5).
7: kĕmôpēt hāyîtî lĕrabbîm — I have been like a portent to many. Môpēt is the word for a miraculous sign or wonder, used regularly of the Exodus signs (Exodus 7:3; Deuteronomy 26:8). The Psalmist's life — marked by affliction survived through divine rescue — has become a public theological exhibit that startles observers. A life of visible divine care makes the man a walking demonstration. wĕʾattāh māḥăsî-ʿōz — but you are my strong refuge.
8: yimmālêʾ pî tĕhillātekhā — my mouth is filled with your praise. The niphal imperfect of mālaʾ (to fill) pictures the mouth completely occupied — no room for complaint or flattery of human patrons. Kol-hayyôm tifʾartekhā — all day your splendor. The time scope is total: no hour stands outside praise, even in a life of suffering.
9: ʿal-tashlikhēnî lĕʿēt ziknāh — do not cast me off at the time of old age. Ziknāh (old age, feminine abstract form) gives this psalm its distinctive character as the elderly believer's prayer. The verb shālakh in the hiphil means to throw away, discard — the fear is precisely that the aging body will be read as divine abandonment. kikĕlôt kōḥî ʾal-taʿăzĕbēnî — when my strength is spent, do not forsake me.
10: wĕhashōmĕrîm nafshî nôʿăṣû yaḥdāw — those who watch for my life take counsel together. Shāmar nafesh (to watch the soul/life) is idiom for those plotting a killing. The conspiracy is organized: they confer (nôʿăṣû) collectively, targeting the vulnerable old man whose reduced social power invites attack.
11: ʾĕlōhîm ʿăzābô — God has forsaken him. The enemies' premise — stated as confident theological analysis — mirrors exactly the abandonment the Psalmist fears in v9. Their conviction that God has left him is what this psalm, by its final verse, will refute. The adversaries are not merely physical threats but agents of theological despair.
12: ʾĕlōhîm ʾal-tirhaq mimmennî — O God, be not far from me. The imperative of rāḥaq (to be/go far) recurs in v18 as a thread through the psalm. Against the enemies' claim (v11) that God has already departed, the Psalmist counters with urgent petition — arguing the opposite of their diagnosis. ʾĕlōhay lĕʿezrātî ḥûshah — my God, hasten to help me.
13: yēbōshû yiklû sōṭĕnê nafshî — may those who are hostile to my life be put to shame and consumed. The imperfects function as jussives — optative wishes, not predictions. Three verbs cover the fate of adversaries: bôsh (shame), kālāh (be consumed/finished), and parallel terms ḥerpāh (reproach) + kelimmāh (disgrace). The inversion: the shame the enemy predicted for the Psalmist (v11) falls instead on the enemy.
14: waʾănî tāmîd ʾăyaḥēl — but I will hope continually. Yāḥal (piel of ḥûl: to wait with tense expectation) is the word of active, strained waiting — not passive resignation. wĕhôsaptî ʿal kol-tĕhillātekhā — and I will add to all your praise. Praise as an ever-growing accumulation; each new rescue adds another layer to the Psalmist's testimony.
15: pî yĕsappēr ṣidqātekhā — my mouth will tell of your righteousness. Ṣidqāh here is God's saving righteousness — his active intervention that makes right what is wrong. Kol-hayyôm tĕshûʿātekhā — all day your salvation. The admission at the end: kî lōʾ yādaʿtî sĕpōrôt — for I do not know the count. The acts of salvation are beyond arithmetic; the speaker cannot enumerate all of them.
16: ʾābôʾ bigebûrôt ʾădōnāy YHWH — I will come with the mighty acts of the Lord GOD. The unusual pairing ʾădōnāy YHWH (Lord GOD) appears frequently in Ezekiel and lends sovereign weight. azkîr ṣidqātekhā lĕbaddekā — I will proclaim your righteousness alone. The exclusive lĕbaddekā (yours alone) forecloses any human co-claim on salvation — the righteousness is God's sole work, the Psalmist merely its herald.
17: ʾĕlōhîm limmadtanî minnĕʿûrāy — O God, you have taught me from my youth. The piel of lāmad (to teach, accustom by repetition — same root as talmid, disciple) — a lifetime of formation rather than a single instruction. The teaching began in youth (naʿûr) and continues: wĕʿad-hēnnāh ʾaggîd niplĕʾôtekhā — and until now I declare your wondrous works. Old age is not the end of the proclamation but its climax, the moment when a full life's witness can be offered.
18: wĕgam ʿad-ziknāh wĕśêbāh ʾĕlōhîm ʾal-taʿazbēnî — even into old age and gray hair, O God, do not forsake me. The two terms ziknāh (old age) and śêbāh (gray hair) form a hendiadys for the full visible condition of advanced age. The prayer is that the proclamation begun in youth (v17) might be completed: ʿad-ʾaggîd zĕrôʿăkā lĕdôr — until I declare your arm to the next generation. The urgency is not merely personal but missiological — the transmission of witness across generations.
19: ṣidqātekhā ʾĕlōhîm ʿad-māroom — your righteousness, O God, reaches to the heights. Māroom (heights, elevated places, the heavens) — God's righteousness is not merely earthly equity but cosmic in scope. ʾăsher ʿāśîtā gedōlôt ʾĕlōhîm mî kāmôkā — you who have done great things, O God — who is like you? The rhetorical question expects no answer; the incomparability formula closes the reflection on divine righteousness.
20: ʾăsher harʾîtanî ṣārôt rabbôt wĕrāʿôt — you who have made me see many troubles and evils. The hiphil of rāʾāh (to show, cause to see) — God himself has been the agent of the Psalmist's exposure to suffering, not merely its permitter. Then the pivot: tāshûb tĕḥayyênî — you will turn and revive me again. The piel of ḥāyāh (to give life) with the adverb again is revival language approaching resurrection. ûmitĕhômôt hāʾāreṣ tāshûb taʿălēnî — from the depths of the earth you will again bring me up — tĕhômôt (the deep places, the underworld) is Sheol territory; the bringing up is early resurrection vocabulary, anticipating Psalms 16:10.
21: tēreb gĕdullātî — you will increase my greatness. Gedulāh (greatness, honor) — an inversion of the shame the enemies expected (v13). wĕtissōb tĕnaḥămēnî — you will turn and comfort me. God turns (šûb) from the posture of allowing affliction to the posture of comfort — a divine relenting toward the righteous sufferer who has remained faithful.
22: gam-ʾănî ʾôdekhā bikelî-nebel ʾămitekhā — I also will praise you with the harp, your faithfulness. The nebel (a large stringed instrument, often translated harp or lyre) marks the vow as liturgically public — this is a sanctuary declaration, not a private murmur. ʾăzammĕrāh lĕkā bĕkhinnôr qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl — I will make melody to you with the lyre, O Holy One of Israel. Qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl is Isaiah's characteristic divine epithet (~30 occurrences in Isaiah); its use here ties this personal psalm of old age to the prophetic theology of divine holiness dwelling in the midst of the nation.
23: tĕrannēnāh śĕpātay kî ʾăzammĕrāh-lāk — my lips will shout for joy when I make melody to you. Rānan (to shout with ringing cry) is the loudest joy verb in Hebrew — used of angelic praise and eschatological triumph. wĕnafshî ʾăsher pādîtā — and my soul, which you have redeemed. Pādāh (to redeem by paying a price) grounds the praise in the specific act of ransom, not in general gratitude.
24: gam-lĕshônî kol-hayyôm tehgeh ṣidqātekhā — my tongue also will meditate on your righteousness all day long. Hāgāh (to murmur, meditate) is the word of Psalms 1:2 — constant, low-voiced reflection filling every hour. The psalm ends where it began: in the righteousness of God, now applied specifically to the enemies' shame: kî bōshû kî-ḥāpĕrû mĕbaqqĕshê rāʿātî — for they have been put to shame, who sought to do me evil. The circle closes.
